text: Welcome to This is My Architecture. My name is Ran and joining me today is Yudha from Fiber. Hi, Yudha.
Hi. Before we get into the architecture, tell us a bit about Fiber. So Fiber helps mobile developers
monetize from ads. Okay, how do they do that? So we do that by connecting by advertisers and
mobile developers. Okay. We are getting half a million ad requests each second and we go out
to advertisers and try to get ads for those ad requests. We go out to advertisers about 15 million
times each second. Okay. Which generate a lot of events. Okay. Tell us how those events look like
and what you do with them. All right. So those 15 million events each seconds go to our Kafka.
We have a Spark streaming application who reads the data from Kafka and put it in S3 in
market format. We have Spark running on EKS triggered by Airflow and aggregating the data again to S3,
S3 to a more aggregated data and also into Cassandra. And also triggers EMR to read the data from S3 and put it into Druid.
Okay. So that's an interesting data flow. You're running your Spark applications on Kubernetes. That's a rather new approach.
Tell us a bit about it and what it solves for you. Our old approach was to run with Yarn, to run Hadoop machines on demand and was not as cost effective.
And we wanted to look for a more efficient way of doing things. So we checked how to run Spark jobs on Kubernetes.
We used EKS to make it faster, not build our own. And it seems to be very cost effective, very stable.
Okay. It seems like you're using SpotInst here. That's an AWS technology partner. What does it do for you?
So the reason this technology is so efficient is because it's auto scaling and using SpotInst.
So we use SpotInst. We have a pod of SpotInst inside our Kubernetes cluster who listen to our metric server and see if we need to spawn more machines up or actually to reduce machines.
Okay. So when Airflow schedules a job on EKS, a Spark job on EKS, SpotInst actually looks at the utilization. If it needs to scale, it increases the size of the cluster with SpotInst.
Yeah. He's listening all the time.
So auto scaling and SpotInst is running with Spark on Kubernetes. That's got to be major cost savings, right?
Yeah. The numbers are about 90% cost saving from old setup.
Great. Sounds really good. So as far as I understand, you have several different data sources filling into your dashboard.
I'm assuming due to different data structure, access patterns. Tell us a bit about those architectural decisions that you made with those.
So those have evolved through requirements from our customers. So in the beginning, they were very humble.
They just want to see how much revenue they made previous day, seven days ago, 30 days ago.
And then they want to know some more data. They want to know how much they make per country, how much they make per application that they own.
And still, Cassandra has a very good setup. You can see a whole year of revenue in milliseconds. Fast, cheap, and very efficient.
And then they wanted more. They want to see, okay, so how am I doing per gender? How am I doing per age?
How am I doing per creative ID, campaign ID, and 50 other more dimensions?
So that's a challenging thing. 50 more dimensions. Even if the cardinality is 10, it's 10 in the power of 50.
So we really scratch our heads and come up with Druid.
So we have Druid with open source called Pivot in the UI.
You can drag and drop different dimensions and you can see revenue per every combination of dimensions that you like.
So those are feeding the dashboard. And what about Presto?
So we have at Fiber a billion users using our systems. We have a lot of data about the users.
So you want to see a snapshot of a billion different users in one second, very fast. This Druid cannot handle.
Druid is very good for data series, not very good for snapshots.
So Presto is actually fit for the job. And actually the people who develop it, Facebook, created Presto for exactly the same purpose,
to power their audience set up in Facebook.
So the right tool for the job. It's reading data directly from S3 because it's a database engine and not a database per se, like Cassandra or Druid.
And it's that.
Great. So I really like the fact that this architecture contains a database engine, like you mentioned,
and different databases which are purpose-built for the job instead of kind of a one-size-fits-all.
And of course, Spark on Kubernetes is the next big thing.
A lot of customers like that. It sounds great that you're doing that with auto-scaling and spot instances.
Thanks very much for sharing.
Thank you for having me.
And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
Thank you.
Thank you.
